elements.sent-2411-001: Community detection is the problem of identifying natural divisions in networks.
elements.sent-2411-002: Efficient parallel algorithms for identifying such divisions are critical in a number of applications.
elements.sent-2411-003: This report presents an optimized implementation of the Label Propagation Algorithm (LPA) for community detection, featuring an asynchronous LPA with a Pick-Less (PL) method every $4$ iterations to handle community swaps, ideal for SIMT hardware like GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-004: It also introduces a novel per-vertex hashtable with hybrid quadratic-double probing for collision resolution.
elements.sent-2411-005: On an NVIDIA A100 GPU, our implementation, \ours{ , outperforms FLPA, NetworKit LPA, and GVE-LPA by $364\times$, $62\times$, and $2.6\times$, respectively, on a server with dual 16-core Intel Xeon Gold 6226R processors --- processing $3.0B$ edges/s on a $2.2B$ edge graph --- and achieves $4.7\%$ higher modularity than FLPA, but $6.1\%$ and $2.2\%$ lower than NetworKit LPA and GVE-LPA.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Community detection involves identifying groups of vertices within a network that have stronger connections to each other than to the rest of the network \cite{com-fortunato10 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: When these communities are determined solely from the network's structure, they are called intrinsic, and if each vertex belongs to only one group, the communities are considered disjoint \cite{com-gregory10, coscia2011classification .}
elements.sent-2411-008: These groups, often referred to as clusters, provide valuable insights into the network's structure and behavior \cite{com-fortunato10, abbe2018community .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Applications of community detection span diverse fields, including ecology \cite{guimera2010origin , healthcare \cite{salathe2010dynamics, bechtel2005lung, haq2016community , machine learning \cite{bai2024leiden, das2011unsupervised , drug discovery \cite{ma2019comparative, udrescu2020uncovering , analysis of biological networks \cite{kim2009centralized, rivera2010nemo, popa2011directed , exploration of human brain networks \cite{he2010graph , and various other graph-related problems \cite{stergiou2018shortcutting, meyerhenke2017parallel, slota2020scalable, henne2015label, gottesburen2021scalable, boldi2011layered, valejo2020coarsening .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Community detection is challenging because there is no prior knowledge about the number of communities or their size distribution \cite{com-gregory10 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: As a result, heuristic-based methods are often used to address this task \cite{com-raghavan07, com-blondel08, com-xie11, traag2023large, clauset2004finding, duch2005community, reichardt2006statistical, com-kloster14, com-traag19, com-you20, com-rosvall08, com-whang13 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The quality of detected communities is commonly evaluated using the modularity metric \cite{com-newman06 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Among these methods, the Label Propagation Algorithm (LPA), also known as RAK \cite{com-raghavan07 , is a popular diffusion-based heuristic that efficiently detects communities of moderate quality.}
elements.sent-2411-014: LPA stands out for its simplicity, speed, and scalability compared to the Louvain method \cite{com-blondel08 , another prominent algorithm recognized for producing high-quality communities.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Specifically, we observe that LPA is $2.3 - 14\times$ faster than Louvain, although the communities it identifies have a quality that is $3 - 30\%$ lower \cite{sahu2023gvelpa .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Although LPA tends to produce communities with lower modularity scores, it has been shown to achieve high Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) relative to ground truth \cite{peng2014accelerating .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In our evaluation of other label-propagation-based methods such as COPRA \cite{com-gregory10 , SLPA \cite{com-xie11 , and LabelRank \cite{com-xie13 , LPA emerged as the most efficient, delivering communities of comparable quality \cite{sahu2023selecting .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this report, we present our parallel GPU implementation of LPA\footnote{\url{https://github.com/puzzlef/rak-communities-cuda , which we call \ours{ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: This implementation builds on our efficient multicore version of LPA, GVE-LPA \cite{sahu2023gvelpa .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In addition to adapting the code for GPU use, we introduced several key optimizations: (1) We employ an efficient hashtable stored in global memory that uses open addressing with a hybrid approach combining quadratic probing and double hashing for collision resolution.
elements.sent-2411-021: As no functional GPU-based implementations of LPA are publicly available, we compared our performance with state-of-the-art sequential implementations like FLPA \cite{traag2023large , as well as multicore implementations such as NetworKit LPA \cite{staudt2016networkit and GVE-LPA \cite{sahu2023gvelpa}\ignore{, as outlined in Section \ref{sec:evaluation .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-025: LPA has also proven effective in automatic segmentation tasks \cite{wang2014geodesic .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Aziz et al. \cite{aziz2023novel apply a modified LPA for sectionalizing power systems, while Stergiou et al. \cite{stergiou2018shortcutting propose Shortcutting Label Propagation for distributed connected components.}}

elements.sent-2411-154: The modularity $Q$ of detected communities is determined using Equation \ref{eq:modularity , involving the Kronecker delta function ($\delta(x,y) = 1$ if $x=y$, $0$ otherwise).}
elements.sent-2411-155: Additionally, the delta modularity for transferring a vertex $i$ from community $d$ to $c$, represented as $\Delta Q_{i: d \rightarrow c}$, is computed using Equation \ref{eq:delta-modularity . \begin{equation}\label{eq:modularity} Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{(i, j) \in E} \left[w_{ij} - \frac{K_i K_j}{2m}\right] \delta(C_i, C_j) = \sum_{c \in \Gamma} \left[\frac{\sigma_c}{2m} - \left(\frac{\Sigma_c}{2m}\right)^2\right]\end{equation} \begin{equation} \label{eq:delta-modularity} \Delta Q_{i: d \rightarrow c} = \Delta Q_{i: d \rightarrow i} + \Delta Q_{i: i \rightarrow c} = \left[ \frac{\sigma_d - 2K_{i \rightarrow d}}{2m} - \left(\frac{\Sigma_d - K_i}{2m}\right)^2 \right] + \left[ 0 - \left(\frac{K_i}{2m}\right)^2 \right] - \left[ \frac{\sigma_d}{2m} - \left(\frac{\Sigma_d}{2m}\right)^2 \right] + \left[ \frac{\sigma_c + 2K_{i \rightarrow c}}{2m} - \left(\frac{\Sigma_c + K_i}{2m}\right)^2 \right] - \left[ \frac{\sigma_c}{2m} - \left(\frac{\Sigma_c}{2m}\right)^2 \right] - \left[ 0 - \left(\frac{K_i}{2m}\right)^2 \right] = \frac{1}{m} (K_{i \rightarrow c} - K_{i \rightarrow d}) - \frac{K_i}{2m^2} (K_i + \Sigma_c - \Sigma_d) \end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation} \label{eq:delta-modularity} \Delta Q_{i: d \rightarrow c} = \Delta Q_{i: d \rightarrow i} + \Delta Q_{i: i \rightarrow c} = \left[ \frac{\sigma_d - 2K_{i \rightarrow d}}{2m} - \left(\frac{\Sigma_d - K_i}{2m}\right)^2 \right] + \left[ 0 - \left(\frac{K_i}{2m}\right)^2 \right] - \left[ \frac{\sigma_d}{2m} - \left(\frac{\Sigma_d}{2m}\right)^2 \right] + \left[ \frac{\sigma_c + 2K_{i \rightarrow c}}{2m} - \left(\frac{\Sigma_c + K_i}{2m}\right)^2 \right] - \left[ \frac{\sigma_c}{2m} - \left(\frac{\Sigma_c}{2m}\right)^2 \right] - \left[ 0 - \left(\frac{K_i}{2m}\right)^2 \right] = \frac{1}{m} (K_{i \rightarrow c} - K_{i \rightarrow d}) - \frac{K_i}{2m^2} (K_i + \Sigma_c - \Sigma_d) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-156: LPA \cite{com-raghavan07 is a widely used diffusion-based technique for detecting communities of moderate quality in large-scale networks.}
elements.sent-2411-157: It is simpler, faster, and more scalable compared to the Louvain method \cite{com-blondel08 .}
elements.sent-2411-158: In LPA, each vertex $i$ initially has a unique label (community ID) $C_i$.
elements.sent-2411-159: During each iteration, vertices update their labels by adopting the label with the highest total interconnecting weight, as described in Equation \ref{eq:rak .}
elements.sent-2411-160: This iterative process continues until densely connected groups of vertices reach a consensus, effectively forming communities.
elements.sent-2411-161: The algorithm terminates when at least a fraction of $1-\tau$ of the vertices (with $\tau$ being the tolerance parameter) retain their current labels.
elements.sent-2411-162: LPA has a time complexity of $O(L |E|)$ and a space complexity of $O(|V| + |E|)$, where $L$ represents the number of iterations performed by the algorithm \cite{com-raghavan07 . \begin{equation}\label{eq:rak} C_i =\ \underset{c\ \in \ \Gamma}{\arg\max} { \sum_{j \in J_i\ |\ C_j = c} w_{ij} }\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Open addressing, also known as closed hashing, is a technique for resolving collisions in hash tables.
elements.sent-2411-164: Instead of using additional data structures like linked lists (as in separate chaining), open addressing manages all entries within a single array.
elements.sent-2411-165: When a hash collision occurs, it is resolved by probing, or searching through alternative positions in the array --- the probe sequence --- until either the desired record is found or an empty slot is located, indicating that the key is not present in the table \cite{tenenbaum1990data .}
elements.sent-2411-166: Common probe sequences include: (1) Linear probing, where the step between probes is fixed (often set to $1$); (2) Quadratic probing, where the interval between probes increases according to a quadratic function; and (3) Double hashing, which uses a second hash function to determine a fixed interval for each record.
elements.sent-2411-167: These methods present trade-offs: linear probing offers the best cache performance but is highly susceptible to clustering, double hashing exhibits virtually no clustering but has weaker cache performance, and quadratic probing strikes a balance between the two.
elements.sent-2411-168: A key factor affecting the efficiency of an open addressing hash table is the load factor, which is the ratio of occupied slots to the total capacity of the array.
elements.sent-2411-169: As the load factor approaches $100\%$, the number of probes needed to find or insert a key grows significantly.
elements.sent-2411-170: For most open addressing methods, a typical load factor is around $50\%$.
elements.sent-2411-171: The fundamental building block of NVIDIA GPUs is the Streaming Multiprocessor (SM).
elements.sent-2411-172: Each SM houses multiple CUDA cores for executing parallel threads.
elements.sent-2411-173: Additionally, SMs have shared memory, registers, and specialized function units.
elements.sent-2411-174: The number of SMs varies by GPU model, with each SM operating independently\ignore{to manage multiple parallel threads .}
elements.sent-2411-175: The memory hierarchy of NVIDIA GPUs includes global, shared, and local memory.
elements.sent-2411-176: Global memory is the largest but slowest, shared memory is a low-latency memory shared among threads within an SM, while local memory serves as private storage for individual threads when register space is insufficient \cite{cuda-sanders10, gpu-nickolls10 .}
elements.sent-2411-177: Threads on a GPU are organized differently from those on a multicore CPU, structured into warps, thread blocks, and grids.
elements.sent-2411-178: A warp consists of 32 threads that execute instructions in lockstep.
elements.sent-2411-179: Thread blocks are groups of threads that run on the same SM.
elements.sent-2411-180: Within a thread block, warps execute together, and the SM schedules alternate warps if threads stall, such as when waiting for memory.
elements.sent-2411-181: Threads in a block can communicate using shared memory, a private, user-managed cache within each SM.
elements.sent-2411-182: A grid, which comprises multiple thread blocks, provides a higher-level structure for managing parallelism and optimizing resource usage.
elements.sent-2411-183: Thread blocks within a grid communicate exclusively through global memory, which, while slower than shared memory, allows for data exchange across different blocks \cite{cuda-sanders10, gpu-nickolls10 .}
elements.sent-2411-184: Our GPU-based implementation of LPA builds upon GVE-LPA \cite{sahu2023gvelpa , incorporating several key features: (1) An asynchronous parallel version of LPA using a single community membership vector, promoting faster convergence but potentially introducing variability in results; (2) A maximum of $20$ iterations; (3) A per-iteration tolerance of $\tau = 0.05$; (4) Vertex pruning to minimize unnecessary computations, where a vertex assigns its neighbors for processing upon label change and is labeled ineligible for further processing once completed; and (5) A strict version of LPA, where each vertex selects the first label with the highest associated weight.}
elements.sent-2411-185: We now discuss our GPU-specific techniques for LPA, below.
elements.sent-2411-186: We first observe that the GPU implementation of LPA fails to converge for a number of input graphs, and instead continues to run for $20$ iterations.
elements.sent-2411-187: This suggests that several vertices are caught in cycles of community or label swaps.
elements.sent-2411-188: A common scenario that can lead to this issue is when two interconnected vertices continuously adopt each other's labels.
elements.sent-2411-189: In general, this can occur when there is symmetry, i.e., when two vertices are equally connected to each other's community.
elements.sent-2411-190: Community swaps, such as these, are more likely than may be anticipated, due to the fact the GPUs execute in lockstep.
elements.sent-2411-191: Symmetric vertices, such as the ones discussed above, can easily end up swapping labels if assigned to the same SM.
elements.sent-2411-192: Since SM assignment is typically based on vertex IDs, this problem can persist indefinitely, leading to the algorithm’s failure to converge.
elements.sent-2411-193: Hence, introducing symmetry-breaking techniques is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-194: To address this challenge, we explore two different methods for symmetry breaking.
elements.sent-2411-195: In the Cross-Check (CC) method, after each iteration of LPA, we validate each vertex's community change to ensure that it is a ``good" change.
elements.sent-2411-196: If not, the vertex reverts to its previous community assignment.
elements.sent-2411-197: We consider a community change to be good if the new community $c^*$ contains a vertex $i$ with that same ID, i.e., $i = c^*$.
elements.sent-2411-198: In other words, a community is considered to be good if all vertices in the community have joined a certain leader vertex, and the leader vertex belongs to the same community.
elements.sent-2411-199: Note how this condition fails in the case of a community swap.
elements.sent-2411-200: Once a ``bad" community change is identified, the community membership of the vertex it atomically reverted to its previous community assignment --- this prevents both vertices involved in the community swap from reverting.
elements.sent-2411-201: In order to be able to perform a revert, we keep of copy of the previous label of each vertex before performing an LPA iteration.
elements.sent-2411-202: We now discuss our Pick-Less (PL) method for tackling the issue of community swaps.
elements.sent-2411-203: In this method, a vertex is only allowed to switch to a new community if the new community ID is lower than its current one.
elements.sent-2411-204: This prevents one of the pairs of vertices involved in a community swap from changing their community membership, thus breaking the symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-205: However, performing either of the two methods too frequently can hinder the algorithm's ability to identify high-quality communities.
elements.sent-2411-206: We experiment with applying either the CC or the PL method every $1$, $2$, $3$, or $4$ iterations of LPA.
elements.sent-2411-207: We do this on large graphs from Table \ref{tab:dataset , and measure the runtimes, and modularity of obtained communities.}
elements.sent-2411-208: We also explore a Hybrid (H) method, combining both the CC and PL methods in all the $16$ possible combinations\ignore{(varying the number of iterations of LPA, after which the CC or PL method is applied) .}
elements.sent-2411-209: Figures \ref{fig:optswapprevent--runtime and \ref{fig:optswapprevent--modularity show the mean relative runtime and relative modularity of obtained communities, respectively, for each of the above discussed methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-210: As the figures show, LPA employing the Pick-Less (PL) method performed every 4 iterations, i.e., PL4, identifies communities of the highest modularity, while being only $8\%$ slower that the fastest method (CC2).
elements.sent-2411-211: This leads us to adopt the PL4 method for our GPU implementation of LPA.
elements.sent-2411-212: Note that we employed a double-hashing based hashtable for the above experiment.
elements.sent-2411-213: A discussion on the design of hashtable is given below.
elements.figure-2411-001: \subfigure{ \label{fig:optswapprevent--runtime} \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{out/optswapprevent-runtime.pdf} } \subfigure{ \label{fig:optswapprevent--modularity} \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{out/optswapprevent-modularity.pdf} } [-2ex] \caption{Relative Runtime and Modularity of obtained communities with different community swap prevention techniques --- these include cross-checking and reverting bad community swaps (CC) every $1$ to $4$ iterations, enforcing picking/selection of only a label with a lower ID value (PL) every $1$ to $4$ iterations, and a hybrid of the two techniques (H) performed every $1$ to $4$ iterations.} \label{fig:optswapprevent}
elements.sent-2411-214: In GVE-LPA, we utilized per-thread collision-free hashtables, comprising a key list and a full-size values array (of size $|V|$) that were kept well-separated in memory.
elements.sent-2411-215: This design achieved a $15.8\times$ performance improvement compared to C++'s built-in std::unordered\_ map.
elements.sent-2411-216: However, unlike multicore CPUs, GPUs operate with a far greater number of threads, making it impractical to allocate $O(|V|)$ memory for each thread on a GPU.
elements.sent-2411-217: This challenge is compounded by the fact that GPUs have limited memory capacity.
elements.sent-2411-218: To address these challenges, we instead opt for a hashtable based on open-addressing.
elements.sent-2411-219: This hashtable $H$ is composed of two arrays: a keys array $H_k$ and a values array $H_v$ --- $H_k$ stores neighboring labels of the current vertex, while $H_v$ holds the corresponding edge weights.
elements.sent-2411-220: The size of the hashtable must be at least as large as the degree of the vertex being processed.
elements.sent-2411-221: However, since a vertex can have a degree as high as $|V|$, allocating a fixed memory block for the hashtable per thread would be impractical, and put us back at square one.
elements.sent-2411-222: Instead, we create a dedicated hashtable for each vertex in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-223: Given that the total number of edges in the graph is $|E|$, the cumulative size of all hashtables can be bounded by $O(|E|)$.
elements.sent-2411-224: In order to ensure a load factor below $100\%$, we allocate twice the degree of each vertex as the size of its corresponding hashtable.
elements.sent-2411-225: This means that memory allocation for all per-vertex hashtables only requires two calls of size $2|E|$: one for all $H_k$s, and another for the $H_v$s.
elements.sent-2411-226: Each thread can then retrieve the hashtable $H$ associated with a vertex, $i$, by computing offsets.
elements.sent-2411-227: This is done using the same offset information provided in the Compressed Sparse Row (CSR) format: specifically, the starting position of the vertex’s edges and its degree.
elements.sent-2411-228: For efficient hash computation, we set the size of each hashtable to $nextPow2(D_i) - 1$, where $D_i$ is the vertex's degree.
elements.sent-2411-229: This enables the use of the $\bmod$ operator as a simple hash function to find the slot index for a given key.
elements.sent-2411-230: Figure \ref{fig:about-hashtable provides a visual representation of this per-vertex hashtable design.}
elements.sent-2411-231: The hashtable supports a few key operations: accumulating weights for matching keys, clearing the hashtable, and identifying the key with the highest associated weight.
elements.sent-2411-232: Deletion of keys is not required for our scenario.
elements.sent-2411-233: To ensure thread safety when accessing and updating the hashtable, we utilize atomic operations.
elements.sent-2411-234: For example, when accumulating weights, we use atomic addition to update $H_k$ and $H_v$.
elements.sent-2411-235: This prevents race conditions when multiple threads attempt to modify the same key's weight simultaneously.
elements.figure-2411-002: \subfigure{ \label{fig:about-hashtable--all} \includegraphics[width=0.86\linewidth]{out/about-hashtable.pdf} } [-2ex] \caption{Illustration of per-vertex open-addressing hashtables for our GPU implementation of LPA. Each vertex $i$ has a hashtable $H$ with a keys array $H_k$ and a values array $H_v$. Memory for all vertices' hash key and value arrays is allocated together. The hashtable's offset for vertex $i$ is $2O_i$, where $O_i$ is its CSR offset. The memory reserved for the hashtable is $2D_i$, with $D_i$ being the vertex's degree. The hashtable's capacity, or maximum key-value pairs, is $nextPow2(D_i) - 1$.} \label{fig:about-hashtable}
elements.sent-2411-236: We now examine three common techniques for resolving collisions in hashing: linear probing, quadratic probing, and double hashing.
elements.sent-2411-237: As previously mentioned, linear probing is cache-efficient but tends to experience high clustering, increasing the likelihood of repeated collisions.
elements.sent-2411-238: In contrast, double hashing significantly reduces clustering but lacks cache efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-239: Quadratic probing falls somewhere between the two in terms of clustering and cache efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-240: It is important to note that repeated collisions can lead to thread divergence, which can severely impact algorithm performance\ignore{--- whether using a thread-per-vertex or block-per-vertex approach .}
elements.sent-2411-241: In our implementation, we set the probe step for linear probing to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-242: For quadratic probing, we begin with an initial probe step of $1$ and double it with each subsequent collision.
elements.sent-2411-243: For double hashing, we use a secondary prime, $p_2 = nextPow2(p_1) - 1$, which is co-prime with $p_1 = |H|$.
elements.sent-2411-244: This secondary prime, $p_2$, is utilized with the modulo operator to calculate the probe step for each key.
elements.sent-2411-245: To further reduce collisions, we consider a hybrid collision resolution technique, which we refer to as quadratic-double.
elements.sent-2411-246: In this method, the probe step is the sum of the steps calculated by quadratic probing and double hashing.
elements.sent-2411-247: Figure \ref{fig:optresolution compares the mean relative runtime of our GPU implementation of LPA using these four collision resolution strategies, with the per-vertex hashtables, on the large graphs presented in Table \ref{tab:dataset .}}
elements.sent-2411-248: As Figure \ref{fig:optresolution shows, the quadratic-double approach delivers the best performance.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Specifically, it is $2.8\times$, $3.7\times$, and $3.2\times$ times faster than LPA implementations that use linear probing, quadratic probing, and double hashing, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-250: This suggests that the hybrid approach effectively balances clustering and cache efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-251: Consequently, we adopt the quadratic-double strategy for our GPU-based LPA implementation.
elements.sent-2411-252: We also tested a coalesced chaining-based hashtable --- a collision resolution technique that combines aspects of separate chaining and open addressing --- utilizing another nexts array $H_n$\ignore{for maintaining the chains needed .}
elements.sent-2411-253: However, it did not improve performance.
elements.sent-2411-254: We also experimented with shared memory-based hashtables for low-degree vertices, but saw little to no performance gain.
elements.figure-2411-003: \subfigure{ \label{fig:optresolution--runtime} \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{out/optresolution-runtime.pdf} } [-2ex] \caption{Relative Runtime with using Linear probing, Quadriatic probing, Double hashing, and a hybrid of Quadriatic probing and Double hashing (Quadriatic-double) for collision resolution in the per-vertex hashtables\ignore{(which is used for storing the total associated weight with each label)}.} \label{fig:optresolution}
elements.sent-2411-255: Processing each vertex in the graph with a thread-block per vertex may not be efficient, especially for low-degree vertices.
elements.sent-2411-256: If a vertex has a degree lower than $32$ --- the warp size on NVIDIA GPUs --- many threads in the warp would remain idle.
elements.sent-2411-257: Since graphs often contain a large number of such low-degree vertices, we opt to handle them using a thread-per-vertex approach\ignore{, a standard practice in the literature \cite{wu2010efficient .}}
elements.sent-2411-258: To implement this, we divide the vertices into two partitions: low-degree and high-degree.
elements.sent-2411-259: Low-degree vertices are processed using a thread-per-vertex kernel, while high-degree vertices are handled with a block-per-vertex kernel.
elements.sent-2411-260: We experiment with different partition thresholds, ranging from a degree of $2$ to $256$, using graphs from our dataset.
elements.sent-2411-261: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:optswitchdegree , a switch degree of $32$ yields the best performance (highlighted in the figure).}
elements.sent-2411-262: Note that in the thread-per-vertex kernel, only a single thread operates on the hashtable.
elements.sent-2411-263: This eliminates the need for atomic operations.
elements.figure-2411-004: \subfigure{ \label{fig:optswitchdegree--runtime} \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{out/optswitchdegree-runtime.pdf} } [-2ex] \caption{Relative Runtime with various switch degrees, i.e., switching point by vertex degree between the thread-per-vertex kernel vs. block-per-vertex kernel, ranging from $2$ to $256$. Vertices with degree lower than the switch degree are processed by the thread-per-vertex kernel, while the remaining vertices are processed by the block-per-vertex kernel (the vertices are partitioned accordingly).} \label{fig:optswitchdegree}
elements.sent-2411-264: Finally, we experiment with using 32-bit floating-point numbers as hashtable values, i.e., for aggregated label weights, instead of 64-bit floating-point numbers.
elements.sent-2411-265: Figure \ref{fig:optdatatype illustrates the relative runtime comparison between the two.}
elements.sent-2411-266: Our findings indicate that using 32-bit floats does not compromise the quality of the communities obtained, while providing a moderate speedup.
elements.sent-2411-267: As a result, we adopt 32-bit floats as hashtable values in our GPU implementation of LPA.
elements.figure-2411-005: \subfigure{ \label{fig:optdatatype--runtime} \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{out/optdatatype-runtime.pdf} } [-2ex] \caption{Relative Runtime with using 32-bit floating point values (Float) compared to 64-bit floating point values (Double) for the aggregated weights (values) in the hashtable.} \label{fig:optdatatype}
elements.sent-2411-268: We now explain our GPU implementation of LPA, which we refer to as \ours{ , in Algorithms \ref{alg:rak and \ref{alg:hashtable .}}}
elements.sent-2411-269: In \ours{ , the symbol $\nu$ is a reference to ``video card".}
elements.sent-2411-270: In contrast, GVE-LPA has a space complexity of $O(TN + M)$, where $T$ represents the number of threads being used.
elements.sent-2411-271: The main step of \ours{ is described in Algorithm \ref{alg:rak .}}
elements.sent-2411-272: Here, the function lpa() takes the input graph $G$ and outputs the community membership (or label) for each vertex.
elements.sent-2411-273: In lines \ref{alg:rak--init-begin to \ref{alg:rak--init-end , we start by assigning a unique community label to each vertex --- effectively making each vertex its own community --- and mark all vertices as unprocessed.}}
elements.sent-2411-274: Next, multiple iterations of label propagation are carried out (lines \ref{alg:rak--iters-begin to \ref{alg:rak--iters-end ).}}
elements.sent-2411-275: As discussed earlier, we use the Pick-Less (PL) method every $\rho = 4$ iterations, where a vertex can only switch to a new community if the new community ID is smaller than its current one (lines \ref{alg:rak--pl-begin to \ref{alg:rak--pl-end ) --- this helps mitigate community swaps.}}
elements.sent-2411-276: During each iteration, unprocessed vertices choose the label with the highest interconnecting weight via the lpaMove() function (line \ref{alg:rak--doiter ).}
elements.sent-2411-277: In line \ref{alg:rak--converged , we verify whether the fraction of changed vertices, $\Delta N/N$, is below the tolerance threshold $\tau$ and PL is disabled.}
elements.sent-2411-278: If it is, the labels have converged, and the algorithm terminates, returning the identified communities in line \ref{alg:rak--return .}
elements.sent-2411-279: Each iteration of \ours{ is performed in the lpaMove() function.}
elements.sent-2411-280: Here, we first initialize the total count of changed vertices, $\Delta N$, the number of changed vertices per thread or thread-block $\Delta N_T$, and allocate space for the buffers $buf_k$ and $buf_v$ (each of size $2|E|$) for the per-vertex hash key arrays $H_k$ and hash value arrays $H_v$ (lines \ref{alg:rak--iterinit-begin -\ref{alg:rak--iterinit-end ).}}
elements.sent-2411-281: Next, we perform the parallel update of labels for each vertex $i$ in the graph (lines \ref{alg:rak--iter-begin -\ref{alg:rak--iter-end ).}}
elements.sent-2411-282: First, we mark vertex $i$ as processed.
elements.sent-2411-283: Then, if the degree of $i$ is less than SWITCH\_DEGREE (set to 32), it is processed by an individual thread.
elements.sent-2411-285: For each vertex $i$, we calculate the size $|H| = p_1$ of the hashtable for $i$, and select a secondary prime number for quadratic-double hashing.
elements.sent-2411-286: The hashtable's memory, $H_k$ and $H_v$, is identified from the buffers $buf_k$ and $buf_v$ (lines \ref{alg:rak--hashtable-begin -\ref{alg:rak--hashtable-end ).}}
elements.sent-2411-287: In line \ref{alg:rak--hashclear , we first clear the hashtable in parallel, preparing for the identification of the most weighted label for $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-288: Subsequently, we iterate over each neighbor $j$ of vertex $i$, accumulating its weighted label $(C[j], w)$ into the hashtable $H$ (lines \ref{alg:rak--scan-begin -\ref{alg:rak--scan-end ).}}
elements.sent-2411-289: Afterward, we determine the most weighted label $c^*$ for vertex $i$ by performing a parallel max-reduce operation on the hashtable (line \ref{alg:rak--maxkey ).}
elements.sent-2411-290: If the PL method is disabled or $c^*$ is smaller than $i$'s previous label, vertex $i$ adopts the new label $c^*$, the count of changed vertices per thread is updated, and the neighbors of vertex $i$ are marked as unprocessed in parallel (lines \ref{alg:rak--domove-begin -\ref{alg:rak--domove-end ).}}
elements.sent-2411-291: Once all vertices assigned to the current thread/thread-block are processed, the global count of changed vertices $\Delta N$ is updated using atomic operations (line \ref{alg:rak--updatechanged ), and returned (line \ref{alg:rak--returnchanged ).}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{\ours{}: Our GPU-based LPA.} \label{alg:rak} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require{$G(V, E)$: Input graph} \Require{$C$: Community membership of each vertex} \Ensure{$\Delta N$: Number of changed vertices, overall} \Ensure{$\Delta N_T$: Changed vertices per thread / thread-block} \Ensure{$c^*$: Most weighted label for vertex $i$} \Ensure{$H$: Per thread / thread-block hashtable\ignore{($H_k$: keys, $H_v$: values)}} \Ensure{$p_1$: Capacity of $H$, and also a prime} \Ensure{$p_2$: Secondary prime, such that $p_2 > p_1$} \Ensure{$l_i$: Number of iterations performed} \Ensure{$\tau$: Per iteration tolerance} \Statex \Function{lpa}{$G$} \State Vertex membership: $C \gets [0 .. |V|)$ \label{alg:rak--init-begin} \State Mark all vertices in $G$ as unprocessed \label{alg:rak--init-end} \ForAll{$l_i \in [0\ \dots\ \text{ {MAX\_ITERATIONS}})$} \label{alg:rak--iters-begin} \State $\rhd$ Mitigate community swaps with pick-less mode \If{$l_i \bmod \rho = 0$} Enable pick-less mode \label{alg:rak--pl-begin} \Else\ Disable pick-less mode \EndIf \label{alg:rak--pl-end} \State $\Delta N \gets lpaMove(G, C)$ \label{alg:rak--doiter} \If{not pick-less and $\Delta N / N < \tau$} break \label{alg:rak--converged} \EndIf \EndFor \label{alg:rak--iters-end} \Return{$C$} \label{alg:rak--return} \EndFunction \Statex \Function{lpaMove}{$G, C$} \State $\Delta N \gets 0$ ; $\Delta N_T \gets \{\}$ \label{alg:rak--iterinit-begin} \State $buf_k \gets \{\}$ ; $buf_v \gets \{\}$ \State $\Delta N_T \gets 0$ on each thread / thread-block \label{alg:rak--iterinit-end} \ForAll{unprocessed $i \in V$ in parallel} \label{alg:rak--iter-begin} \State $\rhd$ Mark vertex $i$ as processed \State $\rhd$ If degree of $i <$ {SWITCH\_DEGREE}, \State $\rhd$ process using a thread, else use a thread-block \State $p_1 \gets nextPow2(G.degree(i)) - 1$ \label{alg:rak--hashtable-begin} \State $p_2 \gets nextPow2(p_1) - 1$ \State $\theta_H \gets 2 * G.offset(i)$ \State $H_k \gets buf_k[\theta_H : \theta_H + p_1]$ \Comment{$H$ is shared, if using} \State $H_v \gets buf_v[\theta_H : \theta_H + p_1]$ \Comment{a thread-block} \label{alg:rak--hashtable-end} \State $\rhd$ Identify most weighted label for vertex $i$ \State $hashtableClear(H)$ in parallel \label{alg:rak--hashclear} \ForAll{$(j, w) \in G.neighbors(i)$ in parallel} \label{alg:rak--scan-begin} \If{$j = i$} continue \EndIf \State $hashtableAccumulate(H, p_1, p_2, C[j], w)$ \label{alg:rak--accumulate} \EndFor \label{alg:rak--scan-end} \State $c^* \gets hashtableMaxKey(H)$ in parallel \label{alg:rak--maxkey} \State $\rhd$ Change label of vertex $i$ to most weighted label $c^*$ \If{$c^* \neq C[i]$ and $($not pick-less or $c^* \leq C[i])$} \label{alg:rak--domove-begin} \State $C[i] \gets c^*$ \State $\Delta N_T \gets \Delta N_T + 1$ \ForAll{$j \in G.neighbors(i)$ in parallel} \State Mark $j$ as unprocessed \EndFor \EndIf \label{alg:rak--domove-end} \EndFor \label{alg:rak--iter-end} \State $atomicAdd(\Delta N, \Delta N_T)$ on each thread / thread-block \label{alg:rak--updatechanged} \Return{$\Delta N$} \label{alg:rak--returnchanged} \EndFunction \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-293: We now turn our attention to Algorithm \ref{alg:hashtable , which is designed to accumulate the associated weights of labels for a vertex within its hashtable, while efficiently handling potential collisions through a hybrid quadratic-double probing strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Given a key $k$ and its corresponding value $v$, the algorithm aims to locate an appropriate slot in the hashtable $H$ (with $p_1$ slots) where the key can either be inserted or its value updated.
elements.sent-2411-295: The process begins by calculating an initial slot index $s$ using the primary hash function: $s = i \bmod p_1$ (line \ref{alg:hashtable--slot ), where $i$ starts as the key $k$ and is adjusted incrementally using a step size $\delta i$.}
elements.sent-2411-296: If the computed slot $s$ is empty (denoted by $\phi$) or already contains the key $k$, the algorithm proceeds to update or insert the value in $H_v[s]$.
elements.sent-2411-297: The approach differs depending on whether the hashtable is accessed by multiple threads (shared scenario, lines \ref{alg:hashtable--shared-begin -\ref{alg:hashtable--shared-end ) or a single thread (non-shared scenario, lines \ref{alg:hashtable--unshared-begin -\ref{alg:hashtable--unshared-end ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-298: In the non-shared case, the algorithm directly modifies $H_k$ and $H_v$.
elements.sent-2411-299: In the shared case, atomic operations ensure thread safety: an atomicCAS() operation attempts to atomically set $H_k[s]$ to $k$ if the slot is empty.
elements.sent-2411-300: If this operation succeeds, or if the slot already contains $k$, the atomicAdd() operation updates $H_v[s]$ by adding the new value $v$.
elements.sent-2411-301: If the slot is occupied by a different key, hybrid quadratic-double probing is triggered as follows: $\delta i$ is doubled and adjusted using $k \bmod p_2$ (line \ref{alg:hashtable--update-end ), where $p_2$ is a secondary prime larger than $p_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-302: This process repeats iteratively (lines \ref{alg:hashtable--loop-begin -\ref{alg:hashtable--loop-end ) for up to MAX\_RETRIES attempts.}}
elements.sent-2411-303: If a suitable slot is not located within the maximum retries, the function returns a failed status --- though this scenario is avoided by ensuring the hashtable has sufficient capacity for all entries.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Accumulating associated weights of labels.} \label{alg:hashtable} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require{$H$: Hashtable with $p_1$ slots ($H_k$: keys, $H_v$: values)} \Require{$p_1$: Capacity of $H$, and also a prime} \Require{$p_2$: Secondary prime, such that $p_2 > p_1$} \Require{$k, v$: Key, associated value to accumulate} \Ensure{$s$: Slot index} \Statex \Function{hashtableAccumulate}{$H, p_1, p_2, k, v$} \State $i \gets k$ ; $\delta i \gets 1$ \label{alg:hashtable--init} \ForAll{$t \in [0\ \dots\ \text{ {MAX\_RETRIES}})$} \label{alg:hashtable--loop-begin} \State $s \gets i \bmod p_1$ \Comment{$1^{st}$ hash function} \label{alg:hashtable--slot} \If{is not shared} \If{$H_k[s] = k$ or $H_k[s] = \phi$} \label{alg:hashtable--shared-begin} \If{$H_k[s] = \phi$} $H_k[s] \gets k$ \EndIf \State $H_v[s] \gets v$ \Return{$done$} \EndIf \label{alg:hashtable--shared-end} \Else \If{$H_k[s] = k$ or $H_k[s] = \phi$} \label{alg:hashtable--unshared-begin} \State $k_{old} \gets atomicCAS(H_k[s], \phi, k)$ \If{$k_{old} = \phi$ or $k_{old} = k$} \State $atomicAdd(H_v[s], v)$ \Return{$done$} \EndIf \EndIf \label{alg:hashtable--unshared-end} \EndIf \State $i \gets i + \delta i$ \label{alg:hashtable--update-begin} \State $\delta i \gets 2 * \delta i + (k \bmod p_2)$ \Comment{$2^{nd}$ hash function}\label{alg:hashtable--update-end} \EndFor \label{alg:hashtable--loop-end} \Return{$failed$} \EndFunction \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-331: In conclusion, in this technical report, we introduced an efficient implementation of the Label Propagation Algorithm (LPA) for community detection.
elements.sent-2411-332: It used an asynchronous implementation of LPA, which employed a Pick-Less (PL) approach every $4$ iterations for handling community swaps --- a scenario more likely to occur on SIMT hardware, such as GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-333: Additionally, it utilized a novel open-addressing-based per-vertex hashtable with hybrid quadratic-double probing for collision resolution.
elements.sent-2411-334: On a server with an NVIDIA A100 GPU, our GPU implementation of LPA, referred to as \ours{ , outperformed FLPA, NetworKit LPA, and GVE-LPA by $364\times$, $62\times$, and $2.6\times$, respectively, running on a server equipped with dual 16-core Intel Xeon Gold 6226R processors --- achieving a processing rate of $3.0B$ edges/s on a $2.2B$ edge graph --- while identifying communities with $4.7\%$ higher modularity than FLPA, but $6.1\%$ and $2.2\%$ lower modularity than NetworKit LPA and GVE-LPA.}
